questions.text: Data that is collected by the investigator himself for a specific purpose is called:
questions.explanation: Primary data is collected directly by the investigator for their specific purpose.

questions.text: Data collected from newspapers, books or other sources is called:
questions.explanation: Secondary data is data already collected by someone else and available from other sources.

questions.text: The mean of first 5 natural numbers is:
The median of the data 3, 5, 7, 9, 11 is:
questions.explanation: The data is already arranged in order. Middle value (3rd of 5) = 7.

questions.text: The mode of data 2, 3, 4, 3, 5, 3, 6 is:
questions.explanation: Mode is the value that appears most often. 3 appears 3 times, more than any other.

questions.text: A bar graph is used to represent:
questions.explanation: Bar graphs represent discrete or categorical data using rectangular bars of equal width.

questions.text: A histogram is different from a bar graph because in a histogram:

options.b: bars have no spaces between them

questions.explanation: In a histogram, the bars touch each other (no gaps) as the data is continuous.

questions.text: The class mark of a class interval 20-30 is:
questions.explanation: Class mark = (lower limit + upper limit) / 2 = (20 + 30) / 2 = 25.

questions.text: The class width (class size) of interval 10-20 is:
questions.explanation: Class width = upper limit - lower limit = 20 - 10 = 10.

questions.text: Mean = (Sum of all observations) / (Number of observations). This formula gives:
questions.explanation: This is the formula for arithmetic mean (average).

questions.text: When data is arranged in ascending or descending order and the number of observations is even, the median is:
questions.explanation: For even number of observations, median = average of the (n/2)th and (n/2 + 1)th values.

questions.text: The range of data 12, 5, 7, 3, 20, 15, 8 is:
questions.explanation: Range = Maximum - Minimum = 20 - 3 = 17.

questions.text: A frequency polygon is drawn by connecting the:

options.a: upper corners of bars in a histogram
options.b: midpoints of tops of bars in a histogram

questions.explanation: A frequency polygon connects the midpoints (class marks) of the tops of histogram bars.

questions.text: If each observation in a data set is increased by 5, the mean will:
questions.explanation: If a constant is added to each observation, the mean also increases by that constant.

questions.text: Which measure of central tendency is most affected by extreme values (outliers)?
questions.explanation: The mean uses all values in calculation, so extreme values (outliers) affect it the most.

questions.text: The mean of 5 numbers is 10. If one number is removed and the new mean is 8, the removed number is:
questions.explanation: Sum of 5 numbers = 50. Sum of 4 numbers = 32. Removed number = 50 - 32 = 18.

questions.text: A data set has two modes. Such data is called:
questions.explanation: Data with two modes is called bimodal. Data with one mode is unimodal.

questions.text: The class mark of the interval 40-50 is:
The sum of deviations of observations from their mean is always:
questions.explanation: The algebraic sum of deviations from the arithmetic mean is always zero.

questions.text: The median of data 5, 8, 6, 3, 9, 2, 7 is:
questions.explanation: Arranged: 2,3,5,6,7,8,9. Middle value (4th of 7) = 6.

questions.text: Find the mean of 4, 7, 2, 9, 8, 5, 6, 3:
Find the median of 12, 18, 14, 20, 10, 16, 22:
questions.explanation: Arranged: 10,12,14,16,18,20,22. Middle value (4th of 7) = 16.

questions.text: The mean of 6 numbers is 15. Five of them are 13, 17, 14, 16 and 18. Find the sixth number:
questions.explanation: Sum = 6 x 15 = 90. Sum of five = 78. Sixth = 90 - 78 = 12.
3 appears 3 times, 5 appears 4 times. Mode = 5 (most frequent).

questions.text: The mean of a data set is 25. If each value is multiplied by 4, the new mean is:
questions.explanation: If each observation is multiplied by 4, the mean is also multiplied by 4: 25 x 4 = 100.

questions.text: Find the median of 15, 35, 18, 26, 19, 25, 28, 22 (arrange first):
The mean of 5 observations is 6. If one more observation 12 is added, the new mean is:
questions.explanation: Sum of 5 = 30. New sum = 30 + 12 = 42. New mean = 42/6 = 7.

questions.text: The class marks of a frequency distribution are 5, 15, 25, 35, 45. The class size is:
questions.explanation: Class size = difference between consecutive class marks = 15 - 5 = 10.

questions.text: The mean of first 10 natural numbers is:
If mean of x, x+2, x+4, x+6, x+8 is 15, find x:
The mean of 11 observations is 50. If the mean of first 6 is 49 and last 6 is 52, find the 6th observation:
questions.explanation: Total = 550. First 6 sum = 294. Last 6 sum = 312. 6th observation = 294 + 312 - 550 = 56.

questions.text: The mean of 5 numbers is 18. If one number is excluded, mean becomes 16. Find the excluded number:
questions.explanation: Sum of 5 = 90. Sum of 4 = 64. Excluded = 90 - 64 = 26.

questions.text: The mean of 40 observations is 20. Later it is found that observation 25 was misread as 52. Corrected mean is:
Mean of 9 observations is 35. Mean of 5 of them is 32. Find the mean of the remaining 4:
questions.explanation: Total sum = 315. Sum of 5 = 160. Sum of 4 = 155. Mean of 4 = 155/4 = 38.75. Recalculate: 9x35=315, 5x32=160, remaining sum=155, 155/4=38.75.

questions.text: If the median of the data 5, 9, x, 13, 17 (arranged in ascending order) is 11, find x:
questions.explanation: For 5 observations arranged in order, the median is the 3rd value = x. So x = 11.

questions.text: A student scores 70, 75, 80, 85 in four tests. What score in the 5th test will make the mean exactly 80?
questions.explanation: Required total = 5 x 80 = 400. Current sum = 310. 5th score = 400 - 310 = 90.

questions.text: Find the mean of 10, 20, 30, 40, 50.
Find the median of 3, 7, 1, 9, 5, 11, 13.
questions.explanation: Arranged: 1,3,5,7,9,11,13. Middle value (4th of 7) = 7.

questions.text: Find the range of 45, 12, 78, 34, 56, 23, 90.
questions.explanation: Range = Max - Min = 90 - 12 = 78.

questions.text: The mean of 8 numbers is 12. Find their sum.
questions.explanation: Sum = Mean x n = 12 x 8 = 96.

questions.text: Find the class mark of the interval 30-40.
Find the mean of first 10 even natural numbers.
The mean of x, x+1, x+2, x+3, x+4 is 12. Find x.
Find the median of 14, 22, 9, 18, 26, 30, 12, 16. (8 values)
Mean of 6 observations is 8. If one observation 10 is removed, find the new mean.
questions.explanation: Sum = 48. New sum = 48 - 10 = 38. New mean = 38/5 = 7.6.

questions.text: The mean of 7, 14, x, 30, 21 is 18. Find x.

cases.case_text: A Class 9 teacher recorded the marks obtained by 10 students in a Mathematics test out of 50: 35, 42, 28, 46, 38, 50, 32, 41, 29, 39. The teacher wants to analyse the performance of the class by finding the mean, median and range of the marks. This will help in identifying students who need extra support.

subparts.text: The mean marks of the 10 students is:
Arranged in ascending order: 28,29,32,35,38,39,41,42,46,50. The median is:
Find the range of the marks.

cases.case_text: A shopkeeper records the daily sales (in hundreds of rupees) for 8 days: 12, 18, 15, 22, 10, 18, 20, 25. He wants to find the average daily sales, the most common sales figure and the middle value to present to his accountant for monthly review. He also notices that on the 9th day, his sales were exactly the same as the mean of the first 8 days.

subparts.text: The mean daily sales (in hundreds of rupees) is:
The mode of the daily sales data is:
subparts.explanation: 18 appears twice; all other values appear once. Mode = 18.

subparts.text: The sales on the 9th day equal the mean of the first 8 days. Find the mean of all 9 days' sales.
subparts.explanation: 9th day sales = 17.5. New sum = 140 + 17.5 = 157.5. New mean = 157.5/9 = 17.5. Mean remains unchanged when the new value equals the existing mean.
